purpose: Consult before defining a glossary term. If a prior published lesson already defines it, adopt that meaning and cross-reference, or declare a glossary `alignment` block that names the prior lesson and the relation (adopt/narrow/extend/distinct-sense/supersede) with a rationale. Never silently redefine a term another lesson owns.

uses.definition: Any arrangement ensuring that a participant's entry into the study is irrevocably committed before their upcoming assignment can be known by anyone in a position to act on it — commitment first, knowability second — so that who enters which group cannot be steered.
The units and records actually included in a particular analysis, which may differ from those initially eligible or enrolled.
One of the groups being compared in a trial. A trial comparing a new routine against sitting quietly has two arms.
A pattern in which measured values or categories of two variables differ together in a sampled dataset; the pattern alone does not specify why it occurs.
Distortion caused when post-entry loss, withdrawal, missing outcomes, or exclusion selects an analysis set in a way related to comparison group and outcome or its predictors.
aliases: bias due to loss to follow-up

uses.definition: Output change divided by input change over a specified interval, with the corresponding output-per-input units.
The property that assignment does not systematically favor one group across repeated randomizations, even though chance imbalance can occur in one realized assignment.
The quantity acted on by an exponent.
A systematic distortion of a study estimate or conclusion introduced through design, conduct, measurement, analysis, or reporting.
The study of the molecules and chemical processes of living systems, including enzymes, membranes, energy use, and biological information.

Withholding knowledge of the assigned condition from people involved in a study, from the moment of allocation until the study ends. Applies separately to participants, providers, data collectors, outcome assessors, and analysts.
A stated limit on the sample, setting, time, scale, or conditions to which a question refers.
A conclusion whose causal language, strength, scope, and certainty are limited to what the integrated evidence warrants.

An operation that establishes a relation, under specified conditions, between indications and values supplied by measurement standards with associated uncertainties, then uses that relation to obtain a measurement result.
In this lesson, the adjustment or estimation of model parameters within defensible ranges so model output fits relevant observations according to stated criteria.

alignment.note: Lesson 6 defines calibration as a metrology operation relating an instrument's indications to measurement standards. Here it is model fitting: adjusting parameters so model output matches observations. Different objects (an instrument vs a model), same aim of making outputs trustworthy.

uses.definition: A culminating task in which a learner selects and connects knowledge from several subjects to address an unfamiliar, bounded problem and defend the reasoning.
An observational study that selects cases with an outcome and comparison controls without it from the population that produced the cases, then compares earlier exposure.
A conclusion that an outcome difference is due to a specified exposure, intervention, or assignment rather than to the alternative explanations left by the design.
The explicit comparison of an outcome under one exposure or assignment with the outcome under a stated alternative.
A contrast between outcomes under two or more specified exposure or intervention alternatives for a defined population and period.
A reasoned conclusion about whether changing an exposure would change an outcome, under stated assumptions and conditions.
The numerical factor multiplying the power of ten in scientific notation.
An observational study that begins with a defined population or exposure groups and compares subsequent outcomes.

A variable at which two arrowheads meet on a path, making it a common effect of two other variables; conditioning or selecting on it can open that otherwise blocked path and induce an association between its causes.

A property of the assignment mechanism, not of the groups that actually resulted: averaged over every way the chance mechanism could have come out, the groups match. Any single study can still be imbalanced.
Two or more proposed explanations or relationships considered as possible answers to the same question.
A signed scalar coefficient describing how much of a vector lies along one chosen coordinate direction.
A unit formed by multiplying, dividing, or exponentiating other units, such as metres per second or square centimetres.
Obtaining a reported computational result from the same input data and documented analysis steps, code, and relevant computational conditions.
A relevant feature of the case, setting, or comparison under which a prediction is expected to hold.
Restricting, stratifying, matching, or statistically adjusting an analysis so comparisons are made within or weighted by values of another variable.

A separate rival factor that varies systematically with the independent variable and could also affect the dependent variable, but is not a consequence on the pathway through which the independent variable produces the response.
For the simple structures in this lesson, a pre-exposure common cause that opens a noncausal path between exposure and outcome.

alignment.note: Extends Lesson 3's design-linked rival factor into the causal-graph account: a pre-exposure common cause opening a noncausal path. The reagent-lot examples of Lesson 3 remain valid instances of this fuller definition.

uses.definition: Distortion of an exposure-outcome comparison by another factor related to both the exposure and the outcome, such that the observed association does not isolate the exposure's effect.
The extent to which contributions from different pieces or lines of evidence are compatible for the specified question after differences in design and scope are considered.
A validity problem in which the measurement operation leaves out important parts of the intended construct.

The degree to which evidence and theory support the proposed interpretation of measurement results for a stated use, including how well the operation represents the intended construct and excludes irrelevant influences.
An idea used to organize related observations, such as alertness or coordination, that is represented through chosen indicators rather than observed directly as a single reading.
A feature outside the intended construct that changes measurement results and can therefore distort the proposed interpretation.
An extra comparison condition included to provide a baseline, known response, or another specific check on interpretation.
A protected route in which data are available only to approved users under stated eligibility, security, and use conditions rather than through unrestricted public release.
Another relevant condition deliberately kept alike across the main comparison so it does not create a systematic rival difference.

A group selected because its members were easy to reach or willing to volunteer, rather than by a chance mechanism giving every member of the sampling frame a known chance of selection. In this lesson it is the standard example of a route that narrows or misdirects who a result is about.
Evidence from a predicted relationship between a measure and another defensible measure of the same or a closely related construct.
A ratio of equal quantities used as a multiplicative factor of one to change unit representation without changing the quantity.
For a right-triangle acute angle, adjacent divided by hypotenuse; on the unit circle, the horizontal coordinate.
The outcome that would occur for a unit under an alternative exposure or assignment, including an alternative not actually experienced by that unit at that time.
A study that measures exposure and outcome in a defined population at one time or over one brief window.
The response recorded to examine whether it differs across levels of the independent variable.
Distortion arising when an outcome is measured, assessed, or adjudicated differently because the assessor knows the assigned condition. Also called ascertainment bias.
Misclassification whose error mechanism differs according to the status of another variable in the analysis, such as outcome classification differing by exposure group.
A description of a quantity type in terms of base quantity types such as length, time, and mass, independent of a particular unit.
The requirement that quantities added or equated have compatible dimensions; satisfying it is necessary but does not prove a model is correct.
A relationship y = kx in which y/x equals the constant k wherever x is nonzero.
Evidence from a predicted weak or distinct relationship between a measure and measures of different constructs.
An expected observation for which competing hypotheses give different outcomes, allowing evidence to count differently for them.
A condition limiting allowed variable values, such as a nonzero denominator or a nonnegative integer count.
The set of input or candidate values for which an expression and task are defined.
The set of inputs for which a function is defined in the stated context.
A scalar formed by multiplying corresponding components and adding; it measures directional alignment.

The precise contrast a study is intended to estimate. In an open-label trial, the total effect of assignment to an intervention package can include ordinary attention and expectations created by that package; a content-specific effect beyond those features requires a comparator that matches them.
A question for which observations or measurements can bear on the answer.
Answerable or assessable using observations or measurements from the world.
A statement asserting that two expressions are equal.
Equations with the same solution set over the declared domain.
One coherent group of observations or studies that contributes a particular kind of support to a specified scientific question.
A study in which researchers assign an intervention or exposure and compare outcomes under the assigned conditions.
An analysis developed after patterns or outcomes are inspected, used to generate or refine ideas and reported as exploratory rather than as a test fixed in advance.
The integer in a power of ten that records the number's base-10 scale; a negative exponent indicates a reciprocal power below 1.
The intervention, condition, characteristic, or experience whose relationship with an outcome is being compared.
The intervention, condition, characteristic, or experience whose relationship with an outcome is being studied.

alignment.note: Adopts Lesson 4's meaning: the condition whose relationship with an outcome is under study.

uses.definition: A combination of numbers, variables, and operations that represents a value but does not assert equality.
The degree to which a study's conclusion carries over to people, settings, and conditions outside the study. Also called applicability or generalizability.
Using a fitted or calibrated relationship beyond the conditions or range that supplied its direct evidential support.
A recognizable way a model becomes inadequate for its intended use, such as an assumption failing, a boundary being crossed, or output disagreeing materially with evidence.
An operation that uses the output of one function as the input of another.
A systematic change to function inputs or outputs that translates, reflects, or stretches its graph.
A rule or relation assigning exactly one output to each allowed input.
The study of biological inheritance, variation, genes, and how genetic information contributes to observable traits.
The side opposite the right angle in a right triangle; it is the triangle's longest side.
A proposed explanation or relationship that leads to expectations evidence can test; developed in the next lesson.
A proposed explanation or relationship from which observable expectations can be derived.
An equation true for every value in its declared domain.
The study of biological defence, including barriers, rapid responses, targeted responses, tolerance, and immune memory.
A pattern in which the main potential errors of one approach differ from and are not caused by the main potential errors of another approach.
In the experiments used in this lesson, the factor deliberately changed between the main comparison conditions.
A conclusion drawn from observations and reasoning rather than something recorded directly.
Systematic distortion arising from how an exposure, outcome, covariate, or other study variable is measured, classified, recalled, recorded, or collected.
A comparison that keeps units in their originally assigned groups regardless of whether they received or used the assigned intervention.
The degree to which independent raters give the same ratings or classifications to the same cases under a stated coding rule and conditions.
The degree to which a study's own comparison supports the conclusion drawn about the people or items that were in it.
A numerical scale on which differences are meaningful but the zero point is arbitrary, so ratios of displayed values depend on the chosen origin.
Evidence from a design in which an investigator deliberately assigns or changes an exposure, treatment, process, or condition and compares outcomes.
A calculated quantity that remains constant under an exact model, such as y/x for direct proportion or xy for inverse proportion.
A relationship y = k/x in which the product xy equals the constant k wherever x is nonzero.
A function such as arcsin, arccos, or arctan that returns a principal angle from a trigonometric ratio.
The multiplier applied to corresponding lengths; areas and volumes scale by its square and cube respectively.
A table or graph using logarithms of both positive variables, in which a power law becomes linear.
The nonnegative length or Euclidean norm of a vector.
Keeping a specified person or role unaware of specified study information, such as group assignment, to block that knowledge from influencing conduct or assessment.
Closeness of agreement between a measured value and the value of the quantity being measured; accuracy is a qualitative concept and is not the same as precision.
The difference between a measured value and an appropriate reference quantity value; it can be described as having random and systematic components in a stated measurement model.
How closely replicate measured values agree when obtained under specified conditions; precision describes dispersion and does not by itself establish closeness to a reference.
Evidence about the existence or details of the entities, activities, ordered changes, and conditions that connect a proposed cause to an outcome.
Evidence that bears on the entities, interactions, sequence, and constraints proposed to produce an outcome.

An intermediate factor through which the independent variable produces some or all of its effect on the dependent variable; unlike a confounder, it is part of the proposed causal pathway rather than a separate rival explanation.
A variable on a proposed causal route from exposure to outcome that carries some part of the effect under the stated model.

alignment.note: Same concept as Lesson 3 (an intermediate factor carrying part of the effect), restated on the exposure-to-outcome causal route used in this lesson.

uses.definition: A property of a measurement result that relates it to a stated reference through a documented, unbroken calibration chain in which each link contributes to measurement uncertainty.
The study of microscopic organisms and related biological entities, including how they grow, change, and interact with hosts and environments.

An allocation method that uses selected baseline factors to favour the assignment that reduces current imbalance. It may be deterministic or include a random component, so the method must be described rather than assumed to be random from its name.
Assignment of a unit to an incorrect category for an exposure, outcome, or other variable.
A condition or simplification accepted so the model can represent selected relationships, such as treating a process as constant, negligible, or well mixed.
The purposes, conditions, scales, and contexts for which a model has a defensible basis of use.
A value, condition, or record supplied to a model for a particular use or run.
The prediction, estimate, explanation, classification, or other result produced by applying a model.
A model quantity treated as fixed during one run but available to change between runs, systems, or calibration choices.
A quantity represented in the model whose value can differ among states, observations, or times.
Evidence about molecular identity, structure, interaction, reaction, or change that can test one or more links in a proposed mechanism.
A systematic analysis across many reasonable data-processing or analytic paths to reveal which choices materially affect a result.

A study of exposure variation created by an external event, policy, rule, or implementation process rather than assigned by the research team; the assignment process must be examined before making experiment-like claims.
A study of exposure variation created by an external rule, event, policy, or allocation process whose assignment must be examined before experiment-like causal claims are made.

alignment.note: Adopts Lesson 4's meaning: exposure variation created by an external process rather than by the researcher.

uses.definition: A control condition expected to show the baseline or no tested response, used to reveal background signal or a response where none is expected.
An exposure or outcome chosen to lack the tested causal relation while sharing important bias-producing conditions, so an unexpected association can signal systematic error.

alignment.note: Lesson 3 defines a negative control as a physical baseline condition in an experiment, expected to show no response. Here it is a causal-inference probe: a chosen exposure or outcome that should lack the tested relation but share the biases. Same logic (an effect here means something is off), different object.

uses.definition: The number multiplying a unit when a quantity is expressed in that unit.
A record of what was noticed or measured, stated so that its object, conditions, and result can be checked.
A study in which researchers do not assign the exposure being compared but record exposure and outcome differences that occur.
An information-bias mechanism in which an assessor's knowledge or predisposition influences a judgment, measurement, classification, or recording decision.
Procedural and analytic information made sufficiently inspectable for another person to understand and, where feasible and permitted, re-run or repeat the work.
A specified procedure or rule that turns a target quantity or construct into an observable record.
A power-of-ten description of a number's rough scale, used here as a coarse prediction rather than an exact value.
A power-of-ten description of a quantity's approximate scale.
Evidence about a response or phenotype in an intact organism, where many tissues, processes, behaviours, and environmental inputs can interact.
The person or process that measures, scores, or adjudicates the study outcome. Blinding this role is the usual protection when participants themselves cannot be blinded.
The response, event, state, or measurement that a study records and compares.
The response, event, state, or measurement a study compares across exposure or assignment conditions.

alignment.note: Adopts Lesson 4's meaning: the response a study records and compares across conditions.

uses.definition: For an ordinary positive ratio-scale baseline, the change divided by the starting value, expressed as a percentage and labelled as an increase or decrease.
The arithmetic difference between two percentages, distinct from their relative percent change.
A ratio expressed per hundred relative to a named whole or reference quantity.

Distortion arising when participants or those delivering an intervention behave differently because they know the assigned condition — for example through changed effort, adherence, treatment sought elsewhere, or withdrawal.
The study of how living structures function and regulate their internal conditions across cells, tissues, organs, and systems.
The evidence line or result that carries the greatest justified influence on the integrated conclusion for the specified question.
A test of whether a result is consistent with an estimate, bound, inverse operation, or contextual constraint; it can expose errors but does not prove correctness.
Evidence about patterns, variation, associations, or effects across a defined group of people or other organisms, settings, or times.
The study of health patterns and their distribution across groups, together with the biological, behavioural, social, and environmental conditions related to those patterns.
A control condition expected to produce a known response, used to check that the system can reveal the signal of interest.
A relationship y = kx^p with fixed constant k and exponent p over a stated domain.
An observable result expected if a stated hypothesis holds under stated conditions.
A dated research plan recorded before the relevant outcomes are known so planned decisions can be distinguished from later changes and exploration.
The proportion of a defined population with a specified condition or characteristic at a stated time or over a stated period.
The designated nonnegative even root, or the unique real odd root, represented by a radical symbol.

Drawing a sample from the sampling frame by a chance mechanism that gives every member a known chance of selection; the simplest version is plain random selection. It supports generalizing from the sample toward the frame's population. Random selection decides who enters a study and is distinct from random allocation, which decides which arm an already-entered participant joins.

An equation asserting that two ratios are equal, for example a/b = c/d. This differs from the statistical sense of proportion as a part-to-whole fraction between 0 and 1, introduced in later data-analysis lessons.

A fitted quantitative relationship of the form y = kx^p, including the direct (p = 1) and inverse (p = -1) cases, proposed for data, identified from an invariant, parameterized, and checked against observations and assumptions.

alignment.note: PREM-SCI-010 defines a scientific model broadly as a selective representation of a system built to answer a stated question. This lesson narrows 'model' to a specific fitted quantitative relationship y = kx^p, judged by fit, residuals, and assumptions rather than by mechanism — an economical summary of how a response scales, not a claim about why.

uses.definition: The fixed multiplier k relating variables in a proportional model.
A cohort assembled before the outcomes of interest occur and followed forward as new outcomes are recorded.
Distortion of the accessible evidence base when a whole study's chance of becoming available depends on the nature or strength of its results.
A measurable or specified property whose value can be expressed as a numerical value multiplied by a unit.
A plane-angle unit defined by arc length divided by radius; a full turn is 2 pi radians.

Using a prespecified procedure that gives each participant known assignment probabilities before allocation, selects the condition through chance, and cannot be predicted. The procedure may use baseline characteristics through stratification or another restriction while retaining a random component.
Allocation by a chance process that gives each eligible unit a known chance of receiving each study condition and makes groups comparable in expectation.
Allocation by a chance process with known assignment probabilities that makes pre-assignment causes comparable across groups in expectation.

alignment.note: Adopts Lesson 4's meaning: allocation by a chance process with known probabilities that makes groups comparable in expectation.

uses.definition: Unpredictable variation that changes across repetitions and contributes imprecision rather than a stable systematic shift; it includes sampling variation and unpredictable measurement variation.
The component of measurement error that varies unpredictably across replicate measurements under stated conditions and appears as dispersion among results.
Assignment by a chance process that gives each eligible unit a defined allocation possibility and tends to balance prognostic factors between comparison groups.
The set of outputs a function produces over its declared domain.
A numerical scale with a meaningful zero, so ratios of values can have a stable interpretation.
An ordered comparison of two quantities by division.
An exponent written as m/n in lowest terms with n positive. Over the reals, it combines an nth root with an mth power; zero and negative bases need the domain checks stated in the lesson.
The possibility that records described as de-identified could still be linked to a person, especially by combining variables with other information.
The acute nonnegative angle between a terminal side and the horizontal axis, used with quadrant signs to interpret a standard-position direction.
The whole, baseline, or starting value used as the denominator that gives a ratio or percentage its meaning.
A publication format in which a journal evaluates the research question and proposed method before results are known and later evaluates execution, justified changes, and conclusions.
The contribution reliable evidence would make to the specified question, considering the match in exposure, outcome, system, population, dose, and timing.
Consistency of scores or measurements across specified replications, such as occasions, raters, or item samples; its interpretation depends on which sources of variation are included.
The extent to which the information in a piece or line of evidence is credible for what it reports, given its design, measurement, conduct, analysis, and reporting.

alignment.note: Lesson 6 defines reliability as the consistency of a measurement across repeated readings. Here reliability is the broader evidence-appraisal sense: the credibility of a whole line of evidence. A measurement can be reliable in the Lesson 6 sense yet sit inside evidence judged not credible here.

uses.definition: Measurement precision when specified conditions are kept the same, commonly including the procedure, operator, measuring system, location, and a short time interval.
An informative test of a scientific claim using newly collected data, with the relation between original and changed conditions made explicit.
Measurement precision when specified conditions change; a useful reproducibility claim names the changed conditions, such as operator, instrument, laboratory, or day.
The set of choices available when designing, measuring, processing, analyzing, and reporting a study, some of which can change the result.
Confounding that remains after analysis because relevant common causes were unmeasured, measured poorly, or controlled inadequately.
Observed response minus the response predicted by a model at the same input.

A random allocation procedure that adds a prespecified restriction to improve balance or another design property. Blocking can keep group sizes close; stratified randomization applies a separate chance procedure within groups defined by characteristics such as centre, age band, or severity.
A cohort assembled from existing records that preserve an earlier exposure followed by a later outcome.
A direction in which the proposed outcome influences the proposed exposure, so the observed association may run opposite to the initial causal story.
Stability of a conclusion across specified plausible changes in assumptions, analytic choices, data, or conditions; the relevant changes must always be named.

The list or route through which a study can actually reach people — a resident register, an appointment list, the room a recruitment poster hangs in. A sample can at best represent its frame, and a frame can fall short of the target population.
The signed length of one vector along a unit direction, calculated with a dot product.
In this lesson, one real numerical value, which may be positive, zero, or negative and has no vector direction.
A multiplicative factor comparing a new linear measure or quantity with its reference value.
The size, duration, resolution, or organizational level at which a model represents a system.
An evidence-building process that frames testable questions, compares explanations with observations, and revises conclusions when the evidence requires it; it is not a single rigid recipe.
A selective representation of a system or process, built to answer a stated question by retaining some relationships and omitting others.
A purposeful representation of selected features and relationships used to explain, predict, or test an idea within stated limits.
A representation of a nonzero number as a coefficient multiplied by an integer power of ten; in normalized form the coefficient has absolute value at least 1 and less than 10.

Distortion produced by how people or items came to be in the groups being compared, rather than by anything done to them afterwards. Steering who enters which arm is the form of it that allocation concealment prevents.
Distortion caused when a rule governing inclusion in a sample or analysis changes the target frequency, association, or comparison.

alignment.note: Generalizes Lesson 5's sense. There selection bias is how people came to be in the compared groups (arm entry, which allocation concealment guards); here it is any inclusion rule for a sample or analysis, including survivorship and attrition. The Lesson 5 form is one case of this broader rule-of-inclusion account.

uses.definition: Result-dependent choice or completeness of outcomes, time points, or analyses reported from within a study that measured or planned more.
A comparison of results for the same target question under justified alternative assumptions or analytic choices, used to show how dependent the conclusion is on those choices.
A check of how model output changes when selected inputs, parameters, assumptions, boundaries, or model structure change.

alignment.note: Applies Lesson 9's idea to models specifically: there, varying analytic assumptions to test a conclusion's robustness; here, varying a model's inputs, parameters, or structure to see how its output responds. Same robustness logic, applied to a model.

uses.definition: Random allocation using one fixed allocation ratio for every participant, without blocks, strata, or covariate-adaptive changes to the probabilities. It is the main introductory model in this lesson, not the only valid form of random allocation.
For a right-triangle acute angle, opposite divided by hypotenuse; on the unit circle, the vertical coordinate.
All values in the declared domain that make an equation true.
Methods for describing variation in data and reasoning carefully from observations to bounded conclusions.

An outcome whose value depends on a person's rating, interpretation, or judgement — such as a self-reported score or a clinician's assessment — as opposed to one recorded by an instrument or automatic process. Subjective outcomes are the most exposed to detection bias.
A selection mechanism in which only cases that remained alive, functioning, successful, persistent, or otherwise observable pass into the analyzed set.
The component of measurement error that remains constant or varies predictably across replicate measurements and appears as a shared shift or pattern relative to an appropriate reference.
For a right-triangle acute angle, opposite divided by adjacent; equivalently sine divided by cosine where defined.
The whole group whose characteristics a study is meant to describe, as distinct from the smaller group that the study actually enrolled.
The population or complete set of cases about which a stated estimate or conclusion is intended to speak.

alignment.note: Adopts Lesson 5's meaning: the whole group a study is meant to describe, as opposed to the sample actually studied.

uses.definition: The quantity that a measurement is intended to estimate, such as the duration of a specified event.
A question connected to observations or measurements that could produce more than one possible answer within stated boundaries.
The degree to which a result can support an inference in a population or setting different from the one in which the study comparison was estimated.
Integration of results from approaches selected because their important potential biases differ and are assumed to be largely unrelated.
A limitation in knowledge about a model, its inputs, parameters, structure, or application that affects confidence in its output.
The circle centered at the origin with radius 1; a standard-position angle has endpoint coordinates (cosine, sine) on it.
A vector of magnitude one used to specify a direction or basis axis.
The extent to which a measurement, estimate, or inference supports the intended interpretation for its stated target and conditions.
Differences or fluctuations in the represented system that can be characterized but are not necessarily removed by collecting more information.
A feature or quantity that can take different values or conditions in a comparison.

A mathematical object that supports vector addition and multiplication by scalars and can be represented by signed components in a chosen basis; only a nonzero Euclidean vector has a unique direction.
